speaker: interviewer
text: Halina Balfour, June 29th, 1995. Mrs. Balfour, you were just saying as a young child in Plaszow, your main concern was to hide from the SS and make yourself invisible.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any idea or recollection of about how old you were at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: I must have been around eight, eight or nine. The war started when I was six. And this-- Plaszow was in 1943.

speaker: interviewer
text: And after being in Plaszow, you came to Schindler's enamel factory.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember how you got there?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your next memory is just being there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just being there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember Oskar Schindler? What he looked like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, sure. Good lord, yes-- not only from then, but he was with us after the war in Argentina practically [INAUDIBLE] well.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll talk about that later. But tell us what he was like when you first encountered him. What do you remember about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I-- you must remember that I was very small. I couldn't really judge a man. He was very good looking. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I remember him practically always smiling. And especially, he didn't look like anyone one should-- one could be scared of.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were never afraid of him?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you do at the enamel factory? Did you have a job?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I was too small. I assume that in some papers, it said that I did something. But as far, as far as I remember at least I never did anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where there other children there with you as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember of your interaction with these children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, as normal as the circumstances would allow. I don't think, you know, it was a questions of games, say, of loud games, or running around. Perhaps we did. I don't, I don't remember. But yes, we, we did play.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you remember both of your parents being at the enamel factory?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember them working and what they did?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. They worked in the factory. But what they did, I haven't got the faintest idea. Because I don't think, I don't think either of them knew anything about, about enamel works.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you in the factory?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't think so. I think I was mainly in the barracks or--

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the barracks like?

speaker: interviewee
text: You're asking specifically what were the barracks like there?

speaker: interviewer
text: What you remem-- what you remember of the barracks at the enamel factory.

speaker: interviewee
text: I think there were more beds than shelves there. The barracks [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] were mainly to separate men and women and to get in as many people in every place possible. So the beds must have been more or less the same as everywhere, as in all the other camps.

speaker: interviewer
text: So were you actually with your mother and father or just your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in the camp with my mother and father but in the barracks with my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any things that they might have talked to you about or spoken to each other about as far as their conditions were concerned?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think everybody, everybody was-- you sort of lived with the idea that either this was a bad dream or that you might not survive the day or the next day. And it of dif-- it, it wasn't [? war. ?] It wasn't very, you know, apt to, to make plans for anything. We just survived the day. And the next day took care of itself.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you said you were in the enamel factory for about six months, you think?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right. And then I don't know what happened. But they sent a part of the people who-- at least, I was sent back to, to Plaszow.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who were you with when you were sent back to Plaszow?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think with my mother, not my father. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But weren't sent to Plaszow for a long time, because as I said, Plaszow was being liquidated. You know, as the war went on, more and more places were closed down. And pe-- the people were sent to others. And we were sent to a Birkenau [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] through-- we stopped on the way, I think, in two, or three, or four small camps. But I don't remember what they were like. And I know they were sort of like two days' stay over. Because I don't-- the rest of the time was spent in the train.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember your time in the train and what it was like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very, very nasty.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe it to us.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] it was almost winter. And those-- the, the trains were animal transport things. There were no facilities for anything inside-- no bathroom, not even a-- I don't think they, they even put in a-- sorry.

speaker: interviewer
text: A bucket?

speaker: interviewee
text: A bucket, bucket. And then it was all mixed, women, men, and very, very full. You could, you could stand up. But you couldn't sit down. And no water and no food, and it was quite a long trip. I don't remember. I remember that we arrived in Birkenau in the middle of the night. But I don't remember how long the trip took-- Several days.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you arrived in Birkenau?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I think that is when they-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I was going back. Can I go back?

speaker: interviewer
text: Absolutely.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was going back to Plaszow and that-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] when they sent the children away. Because I-- that's another thing I do remember because of one of those happenings. One morning shortly before Plaszow closed down, we were called as usual at 6:00 in the morning to the appellplatz. But it was a little different, because-- and it was-- we saw it, because we were surrounded by Germans with machine-- soldiers with machine guns. And you know, I must say that the Germans had a kind of-- I know they must have, have had very good publicity professionals. Because they always found the right song for the right moment. Like when they were taking the children away, you know the appellplatz had all loudspeakers all around. And they were all playing a song called, [GERMAN SPEECH]. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] That is another thing I remember fairl-- quite clearly.

speaker: interviewee
text: Goodnight Mother, Goodnight?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And well, only 13 of us saved ourselves.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I mean exactly that. Some were hidden by their parents who knew about what was going to happen before. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I don't remember very well. But I was told by, by my mother who was there that I went down one of the latrines, one of the holes, and hid there. I don't remember whether, whether this did happen or not. But this is what my mother said I did. So I assume she knows. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I wanted to go back to that. Because that was one of the terrorific things that happened. And I guess that is when the real, the real fear started. And then I guess it never went away until the end of the war. It was-- it wasn't throughout the war-- I mean, not the first part. Not-- perhaps not until that moment. But from that moment on, it did.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go on. Well, and then as I said, we were sent to Birkenau. And there again was the-- [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] first of all, they took us to, to those showers. And at least they, they shaved all my hair off. I don't know what happened to, to most of the people. My mother didn't recognize me, recognize me when she, when she saw me. As a matter of fact, she. she asked-- and that, that is another thing I remember. She said, little boy, did you ever-- did you see my, my daughter?

speaker: interviewee
text: And they-- then they gave us those ridiculous clothes. Because see, every transport that came came with their-- very many didn't come-- even if they came from the camps, they had some clothes. And very many came from-- not from Poland. They came directly from Romania, from Bulgaria, et cetera. And they came with, with their clothes, with their suitcases. And they were all taken away. And most of them went to the ovens. But the clothe were given to, to the newcomers, like summer clothes in the middle of the winter. And then they started the segregation thing-- this right, left, right, left. I was very big for my age. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And somehow, I got to segregated into the right direction. It wouldn't have mattered too much, because there was already an order that the 300 people who were from Schindler's weren't to be touched. But we didn't know about it. And, and it was terrifying. We weren't in Birkenau very long. I think we were there for two or three weeks.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you in a barracks with your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: I guess.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember being with your mother while you were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember being with, with my mother right at the beginning when we came to Birkenau and then through the bath routine, et cetera, shower. But I don't remember-- and then I remember being with her when-- on the train going to Birkenau-- to, going to Brunnlitz. But I don't remember being with her in, in Birkenau. And I don't, for instance-- I never knew why she has the number and my father had that. She had the number, and so did my father. But I never did. So somebody must, must have hidden me somewhere.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe to us what your days were like those weeks you were in Birkenau?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Just being scared, just-- no, you, you can't really explain it. It's-- it's like knowing you're going to-- knowing you're dead, just waiting for the moment when, when you die. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] It's ver-- it's terrifying. It real-- that part really is. Then we were very close to the, to the ovens. And the smell is-- of course, you don't know to start off with what the smell is. But somebody-- there is always somebody who very quick, quickly explains.

speaker: interviewer
text: And someone explained that to you while you were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Somebody told me. I must have asked and--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you believe it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes, I think. I think by the time, I believed, believed anything that, that was happening. Because in the beginning, well, I was very little. And, I can't ima-- even-- children, children can't imagine certain things. You know, children can't imagine dying. Something will happen and death will go way.

speaker: interviewer
text: But when you're 10 or 11 and you've lived through years of this, well, you know it-- it's there. It exists. And since you were a child, you were the most likely to, to go. We knew what was happening in the gas-- in the gas, gas chambers. What would people talk about in a concentration camp? Especially Birkenau, which was not a working camp-- it was the death camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: The pre-- so everybody talked about nothing else but that. And you couldn't, you couldn't help listening or hearing. And it wasn't any longer a question of believing. Because there it was. And there you smelt it. And there you saw it. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] So I suppose I was scared, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what the nights were like? Can you describe that to us?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] No. I guess it was more of the same. Because you just were there. And whenever they called you to go out, you thought-- all you thought was, well, here it comes. And when you're a child, you can't even rationalize it. Because when you're, when you're older, you can rationalize death. But when you're a child, you can't. It's something absolutely terrifying. And you can't even say, well, everybody dies eventually. So sooner or later-- well, you'll miss some nice things. You'll miss some bad things. But eventually, you're dead. You can't. When you're a child, you don't think that way. You just think, this is terrible. This is going to hurt. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Well I guess--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you with other children?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not in Birkenau-- there weren't. Legally, there were no children in Bir-- in Birkenau. There may have been-- I mean, the children who later went to Brunnlitz, to Schindler's last camp. There were, I don't know, 10 or 15 of u, whoever was on his list. They were somewhere around in, in Birkenau. But nobody ever saw them. No, no. You couldn't be seen there if you were a child. You wouldn't last a second.

speaker: interviewer
text: So during the day when people were milling around, where were you? What were you doing?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the barracks, mainly in the barracks, and hidden in some corner or hidden in a bed made up in a, in a-- not a bed because weren't any beds-- just hidden, just sort of nonexistent. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] And then came the great news that we were going to Czechoslovakia, to, to Schindler's.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you hear about that? Do you remember that happening?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I think, I think that my mother came and told me. Because my mother was very worried. i grandmother had been separated in the wrong direction. And my mother couldn't find her. She wasn't in any of the barracks in Birken-- Birkenau. But when they got us together to go to Brunnlitz, she was there, so--

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were back with your grandmother again?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you leave Birkenau and get to Brunnlitz.

speaker: interviewee
text: By train.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember that ride? Can you describe that?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. It's another blank. I don't know why those are blanking, because some very-- some really terrorific things I remember perfectly. And the train ride shouldn't have been that terrorific. And I don't remember it at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what time of year it was-- summer, winter?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. It must have been winter, because we arrived in Birkenau in winter.

speaker: interviewer
text: So were you cold, feeling cold, or?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were always cold. Because the clothes we were given was summer-- I was given summer clothes. And there was never enough food. Sometimes, there just wasn't any food. And there very often wasn't any water on the, on the trip. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] But one survived. That, that was the whole, the whole purpose of the, of the exercise, to survive.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when you finally arrived to Brunnlitz, what was that like?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were barracks. There was also SS, although their role wasn't as important there. Because it was-- Brunnlitz was a munitions factory. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] You lived better. The food was much worse, because that was already '45, '44-- end of '44, beginning of '45. And the Germans were losing the war. And there wasn't enough food for them, least of all for us. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] There was that-- it wasn't that bad. I remember I used to sing. I used to have a very good voice, singing voice. And I used to sing every evening for the whole barrack, our barrack.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you sing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Songs.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of songs?

speaker: interviewee
text: Songs I learned as a child, songs I learned from the maids, romantic songs, whatever.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember, do you remember any of those songs?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, I don't remember any Polish songs. You see, [? Carol, ?] the thing is that I have for years, and years, and years, whenever-- when I, when I was about 15, I decided that to be able to live some kind of a life, I had to forget all that. And then about 30 or 40 years later, I decided, well, it's ridiculous. You can't just erase a whole part of your life.

speaker: interviewee
text: The things is that I did, when I decided to do it, I did erase about 40% of everything that happened. And I couldn't get it back. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So that's where the-- why wouldn't I remember the names of the songs? I just don't. It was one of the things that must have touched me in some way. And I didn't want to remember it. But you do remember singing for the barracks?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, but I do remember singing.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you sang in Polish?

speaker: interviewee
text: I sang in Polish.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the reactions of people there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, the people loved it. It was [? some ?] entertainment in a place where there was no entertainment. And everybody-- there were other people who did something, something funny or somebody else who sang in the evening just to fill up time. You couldn't-- you know, even if you live scared and even if you don't have enough food, you still need something more than just waking up, and going to sleep, and going to work, and waking up, and going to sleep. Because otherwise, people wouldn't care whether they sur-- whether they survived or not. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Well, and then the rest of it is more, more or less heresay, really. Because I told you, my father was sent to Gross-Rosen. He wasn't-- he was with us in Brunnlitz ever. But that's only an aside. We heard that the Russians were very close and the Germans were leaving. But apparently, a short time before, the SS commander told Schindler that he wasn't going to leave any Jew alive. And he was going to take us all to the woods there and have us shot. And I know that Oskar-- I don't know what Oskar did to persuade him not to. But the last memory I have of the Germans was they were running away. They were leaving. But they were still shooting at us. And some people got killed. And then the Russians came. And I guess that was the end of the war from, for us.

speaker: interviewer
text: But before we get to that part about the Russians coming and liberating you, what were your memories of Oskar Schindler at Brunnlitz? And what kind of connections did you have with him?

speaker: interviewee
text: I baked a cake for his birthday once.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe that to us.

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't, because I can't even imagine where I could have baked it. Somebody must have taken me to the main kitchens.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you remember baking the cake, having a cake for his birthday?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you present it?

speaker: interviewee
text: But it's a very vague memory.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you present it to him?

speaker: interviewee
text: I haven't got the vaguest idea. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Some-- you see, some of it is hearsay, things in my mother told me I did. So I'm not quite sure whether I remember baking it or whether I remember my mother telling me that I baked it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember presenting him with the cake?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Having a birthday party for him?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, which is why I say they are hearsay memories.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you do remember seeing him at Brunnlitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes, yes-- Him and her.

speaker: interviewer
text: His wife as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was she like?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. She's nuts. She lives in Argentina.
